name: Legal Studies Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a minor the entry publishes no parsed plan of study (requirementGroups=0), so the 41 referenced courses read as an undifferentiated menu rather than a curated path. There is a genuine and distinctive law-and-technology spine -- IT 331 Privacy and Information Technology, IT 332 Digital Crime, IT 400 Information Technology and the Law, MGMT 290 Business Law I, IE 447 Legal Aspects of Engineering, and a deep HIST law bench (HIST 320 Law and Evidence, HIST 342 Civil Rights and Law, HIST 369 Law and Society, HIST 384 Invention and Regulation). Coherence is undercut, however, by heterogeneous science courses in the same pool (CHEM 222 Analytical Chemistry, FRSC 480 Forensic Microscopy, FRSC 359) whose fit to a legal-studies minor is unclear, suggesting the list pools the whole department rather than a legal-studies track.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a short required core (e.g. one foundational law survey plus a choice of law-in-context electives) and separate the law-relevant courses from the forensic/chemistry courses that appear to be included only because they share the department.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 6, driven by the IT security ladder (IT 120 -> IT 340 -> IT 430 Ethical Hacking) and the forensic science chain (CHEM -> FRSC 480), which over-constrain a minor whose law courses themselves need only ENGL 102 plus a 200-level History/Humanities GER. missingCourseCount is 2 -- GER 200 is a requirement-category placeholder rather than a real course, and HIST 312 is a genuine dangling reference. The law courses form a shallow, sensible gate; the deep chains come from science electives that a non-major minor is unlikely to reach.
dimensions.suggestion: Flag or trim the deep IT-security and forensic prerequisite chains for minor students, and resolve the dangling HIST 312 reference (or remove it if it is no longer offered).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview statement appear in the data. For an interdisciplinary minor drawing on history, IT, business, and engineering law, the absence of an outcomes statement leaves students no way to see what competency the minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 explicit learning outcomes (e.g. reading legal sources, reasoning about law-technology conflict, understanding regulatory/privacy frameworks) and a one-paragraph overview of the minor's purpose.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, there are no requirementGroups, and creditReconcileDelta is null -- expected for a minor and noted here as a data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but it does mean the catalog page shows no total credits, no required/elective split, and no cap, so a student cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they are distributed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and the required-vs-elective breakdown so students can plan it against their major.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and informative, but the entry is a flat course inventory with no term structure, no required core marked, and two placeholder/dangling references (GER 200, HIST 312). The law-technology strength is real but buried in a long undifferentiated list that also contains chemistry and forensic-lab courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Restructure the page into labeled sections (foundations, law-and-technology electives, law-in-history electives) and remove the science-lab courses that do not belong to a legal-studies path.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The technology-law emphasis is a genuine, current differentiator for a legal-studies minor at a technological university: IT 331 Privacy and Information Technology, IT 332 Digital Crime, IT 400 IT and the Law, IT 330 Computer Forensics, plus HIST 384 Invention and Regulation and HIST 370 Legal Issues in the History of Media give students exposure to the fastest-moving areas of law. The gap is the near-total absence of explicit intellectual-property/patent law and any AI-governance content, which a tech-facing legal minor should own.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an intellectual-property / patent-law course and an emerging AI-and-algorithmic-accountability offering to complete the technology-law profile.

descriptionQuality.justification: Across the sampled courses descriptions are consistently present and substantive -- HIST law courses, IT 331/332/400, and MGMT 290 each give a clear scope of topics, and none read as thin or placeholder. A few science electives (CHEM 222, FRSC 480) are well described but out of scope for the minor's stated subject.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit with grade gates (e.g. IT 430 'IT 340 or equivalent', CHEM 222 chained C-or-better conditions), but clarity is weakened by the GER 200 placeholder used as if it were a course, the dangling HIST 312, and the many law courses sharing the identical 'ENGL 102 + 200-level History/Humanities GER' string, which does not distinguish their real readiness levels.

missingTopics.topic: Intellectual property, patent, and copyright law
missingTopics.rationale: A technological university's legal minor should foreground IP; only Business Law I (MGMT 290) and IE 447 touch commercial/engineering law, with no dedicated IP or patent course among the 41 references.

missingTopics.topic: Constitutional law and civil liberties fundamentals
missingTopics.rationale: The pool is rich in applied and historical law (HIST 342, HIST 364) but lacks a foundational constitutional-law course to anchor students' understanding of rights and government power.

missingTopics.topic: Contracts, torts, and legal procedure basics
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond MGMT 290 Business Law I there is no systematic exposure to the core private-law doctrines (contracts, torts, procedure) that a legal-studies credential is expected to introduce.

missingTopics.topic: Legal research and writing as a skills course
missingTopics.rationale: HIST 395 Research Methods in Law and Society is close, but a dedicated legal-research/writing course (comparable to STS 300 Legal Reasoning offered elsewhere in the college) would give the minor a transferable skills anchor.
No mediation/arbitration/negotiation content appears, though it is now standard in undergraduate legal-studies curricula and pairs naturally with the program's business-law courses.

emergingTrends.rationale: IT 331 Privacy and IT 332 Digital Crime position the minor to add a course on AI regulation, liability, and algorithmic decision-making, now central to legal practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-privacy regulation (GDPR/CCPA) and cross-border data law
emergingTrends.rationale: IT 331 already teaches privacy foundations; a modern legal minor should extend it to the fast-evolving statutory privacy regimes that dominate technology-law careers.
With IT 330 Computer Forensics in the pool, the minor is well placed to add legal-tech/e-discovery content reflecting how litigation is actually conducted today.
The IT security ladder (IT 230, IT 330) provides the technical base for a law-facing course on breach liability, compliance, and cybercrime prosecution.
EVSC 335 Environmental Law and HIST 375 Legal Issues in Environmental History already exist; climate litigation and regulation are a growth area the minor could brand around.

topSuggestions: Publish an explicit required core, a total-credit requirement, and learning outcomes so the minor reads as a defined credential rather than a departmental course dump.
Add a dedicated intellectual-property/patent-law course -- the most conspicuous gap for a legal minor at a technological university.
Separate the genuine legal-studies electives from the forensic-science and chemistry courses that currently share the pool.
Resolve the dangling HIST 312 reference and convert the GER 200 placeholder into a labeled requirement category.
Introduce an AI-governance / algorithmic-accountability elective to modernize the strong existing technology-law bench.
